data.title: Snap's Quiet Profit Inflection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am initiating coverage on Snap Inc. (SNAP) as the market is focused on old issues like user growth slowdown, regulatory news, and ad demand volatility, but missing the new reality of SNAP’s evolution to a more profitable and cash-flowing business model. While the shares have been impacted by the general negative sentiment, I believe the market has underestimated SNAP’s efforts to transition to a more profitable and cash-flowing business model, which supports my Strong Buy rating for the stock.

Snap Targets 60%+ Gross Margins and Stabilized FCF in 2026

As I see it, Snap has a major shift in its financials as it is moving from prioritizing user acquisition to prioritizing unit economics and cash flow. This shift has a positive force influencing Snap business's forward financials. It is the divergence between infra expenditures and topline combined with the rapid scaling of non-advertising topline.

In Q4-FY25, Snap delivered a gross margin of 59%. This is an increase from 55% in Q3 and 57% in Q4-FY24. This expansion happened because topline growth of 10% YoY outpaced the growth of adjusted cost of revenue. Adjusted cost of revenue rose by 4%. Snap has a target to exceed a 60% gross margin in FY26. The elements behind this expansion involve a strict calibration of infrastructure costs. Infra cost per Daily Active User (DAU) settled at $0.86.

Snap kept these costs below the upper end of guidance ranges. By aligning server and bandwidth expenditures with the monetization reality of specific geographies, Snap prevents low-monetization regions from diluting the overall bottom line.

In my view, this operational discipline converts top-line revenue into bottom-line cash flow. Adjusted EBITDA reached $358 million, marking an $82 million improvement YoY. The flow-through rate (the portion of incremental revenue converting to adjusted EBITDA) is standing at 51%. This high conversion rate means that the business has established a fixed cost base capable of scaling. As revenue rises, a majority of that capital now falls to the bottom line in place of being consumed by variable costs.

As a result, Snap hit a positive net income of $45 million. It is a reversal from near breakeven/loss positions in previous periods. FCF for Q4 totaled $206 million. Over the TTM, operating cash flow hit $656 million. The current P/OCF valuation against the sector median seems in line with operating cash flow growth (YoY) at 58.69%.

Moreover, I view that diversification of topline sources further secures this financial base. The Other Revenue line item based on Snapchat+ subscriptions and Memories storage plans also grew 62% YoY to $232 million. Subscribers increased 71% to reach 24 million, and this revenue differs from ad revenue. It is recurring and predictable, and also it is less sensitive to seasonal marketing budget fluctuations and macro cycles.

By converting 24 million users into paying customers, Snap creates a floor for topline growth that exists independently of ad impressions. Further, these paid features (such as storage plans) utilize existing infrastructure. Converting a cost center (data storage) into a revenue generator (paid archiving) directly boosts gross margins.

On the advertising side, Snap expanded its active advertiser base by 28% YoY. This expansion is based largely on small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs). In my opinion, SMBs offer higher retention and diversification than large enterprise contracts. A larger base reduces concentration risk. If one large retailer reduces spending, the impact can be balanced by thousands of smaller accounts.

Thus, the introduction of automation tools reduced the cost per action for pixel purchase conversions (by 55% in testing). This performance boost indicates that the platform is more attractive to direct-response advertisers (that demand measurable returns). I believe that forward financials may benefit from this structure through compounding efficiency. As Snap enters FY2026, the guidance projects infra costs between $1.6 billion and $1.65 billion. The lower end of this range represents flat costs YoY.

Looking forward, if topline continues to grow at the projected rates (consensus estimates showing double-digit growth for upcoming years), the fixed nature of these costs supports continued margin expansion. The authorized $500 million stock buyback program indicates management believes the cash flow is durable enough to return capital and maintain a strong balance sheet holding $2.9 billion in liquidity with no major debt maturity in FY26 and FY27. The shift toward profitability also involves personnel costs.

Personnel costs grew 8%, and this is largely due to a 7% increase in headcount. However, Snap may keep FY26 headcount growth in line with this level as it is focusing on hiring strictly based on strategic priorities. This prevents OpEx from ballooning and eroding the gross margin gains. The adjusted OpEx guidance of ~$3 billion for FY26 sets a clear limit. With strong revenue projected by Street analysts, the bottom line can be modeled with higher probability than in previous years.

Valuation: Discounted on Forward Metrics Despite Margin Inflection

SNAP has a forward earnings multiple of 9.9x FY1 and 8.15x FY2, which is substantially lower than the multiple of 21x/18x for Meta (META), 25x/20x for Netflix (NFLX), and 21x/17x for Reddit (RDDT). Even on a growth-adjusted basis, the forward PEG multiple of 0.40 for Snap is more attractive compared to the PEG multiple of 1.04 for Meta and 1.13 for Netflix, indicating the market is underestimating the earnings growth acceleration of Snap.

On the revenue side, SNAP has a forward EV/sales multiple of just 1.40x, which is substantially lower compared to the multiple of 6-8x for the peers—Meta, Netflix, and Reddit—and slightly lower than the multiple of 1.70x for Pinterest. The forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.97x is again lower compared to the peers, despite the expansion of gross margins to 60%.

Therefore, applying a 2x forward sales multiple to FY2026 revenue of $6.71 billion implies a valuation that supports a $7–$8 price target, based on 1.695 billion diluted shares outstanding.

What Could Challenge the Bull Thesis

The major risk Snap is currently facing is the contraction of its DAU base because of a regulatory issue and a reduction in growth marketing. As a result, there has been a limit on ad inventory, as well as costs. These costs may affect Snap's financial efficiency. In Q4, Snap's global DAUs fell by 3 million QoQ to 474 million. This is a reversal of the trend of increasing user growth.

Snap’s management attributed a portion of this decline to platform-level age verification in Australia. This single compliance action resulted in the removal of ~400,000 accounts. Although, management stated revenue from under-18 users is not material, the reduction in overall reach affects the network effects that support the platform. As per my view, social platforms depend on density; when users leave, the utility for their friends decreases, which risks more organic churn.

Further, this issue extends beyond Australia. There is an active discussion regarding social media bans for children under 15/16 in the Czech Republic, Turkey, Spain, Greece, Denmark, and the United Kingdom. France’s lower house has already passed a bill. Thus, as these regulations expand country by country, Snap may be facing a fractured operating impact (industry-wide). The regulations require a distinct technical implementation for each jurisdiction.

As a result, it may be increasing engineering overhead and legal expenses. The adjusted OpEx rose 8% in Q4, partly based on legal and regulatory compliance costs. If more countries (or US states) enact strict verification/bans, these non-productive costs will rise and will be eating into the operating leverage of Snap.

If the trend in DAU declines continues or operating leverage doesn’t improve due to regulatory/macro pressures, my view would be reevaluated. However, based on the current trend in margins and the discount in the stock’s valuation, my Strong Buy recommendation remains unchanged.

Takeaway

It’s no longer about growth at all costs; it’s about operating leverage. The margins are expanding, cash is compounding, and revenue is diversifying away from ads. The infrastructure spend is in control, the buybacks are underway, and the incremental revenue is flowing all the way down to the bottom line. Investors are paying for a turnaround, not resiliency, at these prices.

data.title: Intuit: Investors Fear AI -- But AI Is Exactly What Makes It A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intuit Inc. (NASDAQ: INTU)

has demonstrated solid growth in both revenue and FCF over the last years. However, its stock price collapsed YTD by 33% and over 45% from its all-time high, as investors are concerned that AI could render INTU’s software solutions obsolete.

The ironic part is that the company leverages AI agents to offer a “done-for-you” experience and achieve higher average revenue per user (ARPU). The present article examines this paradox and whether investors are being misled by the overall sell-off in the software industry.

Pricing policy and customer stickiness could drive the company’s stock performance at least over the mid-term (2-3 years), but there are significant risks that need to be faced, such as macroeconomic conditions and competition -including the US government.

Financials Remain Strong Despite Stock’s Decline

Before presenting the financial analysis, I will briefly outline INTU’s business model. Intuit operates under two segments: (1) Global Business Segment (GBS) and (2) Consumer Segment (CS).

Global Business Segment

GBS accounts for 77% of its total revenue as of Q1’26 and offers end-to-end financial solutions for small and mid-market businesses. The main target is accounting professionals who assist and advise them with its flagship product, QuickBooks, but also offer all-in-one solutions such as payroll and time tracking, as well as, money solutions, such as merchant payment processing, bill pay, checking accounts and financing for small enterprises.

Moreover, with Intuit Enterprise Suite, the company aims to expand the aforementioned solutions to mid-market companies, something like a new‑generation ERP platform. Finally, GBS includes Mailchimp, a platform offering marketing automation solutions and customer‑relationship tools.

QuickBooks is available either as a Desktop version (installed on the accountant’s PC) -including Desktop services such as support, add-ons and maintenance - or online. Thus, the company leverages on a mixed revenue stream of recurring and fee‑based revenue.

Consumer Segment

This segment primarily serves consumers and professional accountants (again), and includes three products:

  1. TurboTax, which helps consumers complete their taxes, as well as offers a variety of money products, such as early refund access to any bank.
  2. Credit Karma, a personal finance assistant, that provides personalized recommendations for credit cards, home, auto, and personal loans, as well as insurance products. Moreover, it offers access to saving and checking accounts; aiming to help members understand their financial/credit health and achieve their financial goals.
  3. ProTax helps professional accountants in the U.S. and Canada with tax preparation activities.

Financial Analysis

The company demonstrated impressive results in Q1’26, with total YoY revenue up by 18%, while operating and net income were up by 97% and 126%, respectively.

Main growth driver for GBS is the QuickBooks Online platform and overall, its Online ecosystem. On the other hand, the Desktop ecosystem seems to experience minor growth, indicating that revenue is shifting from desktop solutions towards online ones.

Another factor supporting the shift toward online solutions is the growth of the Online Ecosystem, both in terms of customer numbers and pricing policy, which in turn has resulted in higher margins, as discussed later.

Regarding CS, revenue grew by 21% YoY, primarily driven by the Credit Karma product, while TurboTax and ProTax generated limited revenue according to the company; Nevertheless, a 15% YoY increase is considered a more than solid result.

Regarding profitability, INTU continues to improve its margins, as reflected by a 32% operating margin in Q1’26, compared to 29% in Q1’25.

This achievement came despite increased COGS and operating expenses in absolute values. It is likely that AI agents and experts may require additional investment in order to integrate into INTU’s products; however, the company’s pricing power and automated solutions help offset other costs. As a result, the gross margins are expected to continue increase over the following quarters.

Furthermore, operating expenses have been reduced, as a percentage of total revenue, supporting the company’s higher operating margins, despite the increased SGA expenses, driven by 250 sales reps and AI-development capabilities. Nevertheless, the company’s pricing power and cross‑selling can overcome these expenses.

All the above have resulted in an 11.48% profit margin in Q1’26, compared to 6% in Q1’25, signaling an efficient business model that not only can survive the “AI threat” but can continue to grow. To be more precise, an FCF margin of 15% and net debt of $3.17B in Q1’26 are considered acceptable, given that most of its debt is considered long-term and does not put pressure on the company’s liquidity or profitability.

How INTU Has Benefited From AI-driven Automation

INTU highlighted three main growth catalysts -called Big Bets- which are:

  • delivering done-for-you experiences for its customers
  • accelerating money benefits by putting money at the center
  • expansion in mid-sized businesses

Let me break down how these catalysts and AI have given actual value to its clients, with “2.8 million customers leveraging these agents to do the work for them”.

First, INTU has enhanced its solutions with AI agents capable of completing approximately 80–90% of simple tax filings automatically, which is crucial for both professionals and individuals. Experts provide additional information or handle more complex tasks. These done-for-you solutions extend beyond tax workflows, by also integrating payments, tax professionals, capital management, and payroll.

Furthermore, through AI, another feature of salaries calculation is performed, with an expert review when considered necessary (e.g. with tax policy alterations). Moreover, if a business needs additional capital, QuickBooks already has all the financial information (i.e. cash flows and profits) and can offer loan suggestions. It’s obvious that INTU offers end-to-end solutions for SMB; therefore, tax professionals through the ProTax product already have a “clean” tax preparation for their clients and are almost ready.

Having said that, tax professionals who already use QuickBooks are more likely to suggest INTU’s products to their clients in order to have a “single source of information”. Additionally, consumers do not have to worry about tax workloads, since AI and experts prepare their tax filings.

All of the above, create an ecosystem that enhances client stickiness and gives INTU pricing power. From a personal perspective, I would prefer to remain on a trusted platform that already holds my tax history and is able to complete my taxes each year, requiring minimum manual action from me. Especially, as a person with only basic knowledge of tax laws and regulations.

Notably, this is the company’s statement for SMB, which supports my view:

We also launched a sales tax agent, which automatically helps businesses stay compliant. The combination of AI and HI is resonating with QuickBooks Live customer growth of 61% in Q1… The combination of AI and HI is resonating with QuickBooks Live customer growth of 61% in Q1… We're seeing strong momentum in the areas that matter most, with TurboTax Live revenue growth of 51%.

Considering the above, it is now clearer what the company’s second bet of “putting money at the center” means. SMBs have seen actual results regarding payments, cash flows, capital allocation, and overall financial health; for this reason, Average Revenue Per Customer (ARPC) increased by 14%, reflecting both customer stickiness and pricing power.

According to the company’s statement:

We saw total online payment volume for our payments and bill pay customers grew 29%, reflecting continued momentum helping our customers get paid faster and better manage their cash flow.

The third bet is its expansion into mid-sized companies. The company developed new AI-native platforms, targeting to provide ERP solutions for more complex workloads. Specifically, the company stated:

We marked the 1-year anniversary of Intuit Enterprise Suite (IES), our AI-native ERP platform that is disrupting the mid-market… And finally, we unveiled Intuit Accountant Suite, an AI-native offering that will transform accounting firm's efficiency and effectiveness in managing their clients, firm and workforce, all to fuel their success.

Main drivers for this expansion are the QuickBooks (QBO) Advanced in combination with IES. Despite the impressive results and high rate of adoption, as mentioned by the company, expansion is still at an early stage. It is without a doubt that INTU has the know-how to expand its operation into a new market, and this could be a major growth catalyst - especially because its solutions are AI-negative and not “adds-on based” as many peers offer. However, this catalyst is the most sensitive, in my opinion.

Having said that, focusing on the company’s actual results, expansion of about 40% revenue growth and 50% more contracts are indeed promising results, despite the comparison starting on a small basis. Importantly, the company stated:

Turning to mid-market. We continue to make strong progress serving larger and more complex customers with approximately 40% growth for our online ecosystem revenue for QBO Advanced and Intuit Enterprise Suite in Q1… Forrester study estimating that customers can see nearly 300% return on investment over 3 years when using Intuit Enterprise Suite. This value proposition is resonating. For example, a large customer with over 200 entities that we signed in Q4 quickly realized the value of our platform and expanded their contract to include an additional 46 entities in Q1… In aggregate, the total number of IES contracts at the end of the quarter was nearly 50% higher than it was at the end of Q4.

Overall, in my view, AI does not constitute a threat to INTU’s revenue, on the contrary, it is a major component that drives its growth. The company aims to be an all-in-one-platform for its clients, with AI capabilities and automated solutions enhanced, if needed, by expert judgment. Specifically, SMBs can benefit not only from AI, but also from the fact that INTU’s solutions can replace 7-25 apps that SBMs use, meaning reduced cost for them.

Considering the above points, these factors support customer retention and enhance INTU pricing power, since switching cost and "trust" in the company are major components, that clients would effectively need to “pay” to leave INTU’s ecosystem. In addition, the company’s business model creates cross-selling opportunities that support its growth trajectory.

Risk Analysis and Valuation

In my view, the company’s main risk is the high regulatory landscape. The US government in FY24 created the IRS Direct File, which is a free tax preparation and filling platform, for simple and basic tax registrations. Currently, IRS Direct File is eliminated by the government; however, analysts estimated that Intuit lost about 1M users of its free TurboTax edition in FY24.

Despite INTU not having experienced lost revenue - due to these tax filings being simple and free by law - the decrease of users has resulted in fewer cross-selling opportunities, as well as shrinking customers’ stickiness. Therefore, if the current government, or the next one, reconsiders the IRS Direct File, it could affect INTU’s performance. For this reason, I believe INTU is a good option for a portfolio with a mid‑term horizon, after which investors would need to re assess this risk factor.

A second risk factor, which needs monitoring, is competition. INTU offers end-to-end solutions to its clients and has various revenue streams, but Xero (XROLF) -in the SMB accounting market – and H&R Block (HRB) - in tax preparation market – could put margin pressures on INTU or create barriers on gaining more market share. From my perspective, this does not constitute the company’s main risk due to its tech and ecosystem moat.

Finally, AI agents and experts, as mentioned above, create growth opportunities but also require additional costs to operate. If the company cannot scale, its operation could have a direct negative impact on its profitability.

One step before valuation, and in combination with the above-mentioned profitability, I would like to highlight that INTU’s Mailchimp product is shrinking. And to be honest, I am not sure how it will achieve double-digit growth in FY26. Therefore, this is a risk that investors also need to consider, given that it affects the company’s overall performance. Notably, management stated:

Global Business Solutions Group revenue grew 18% during the quarter or 20% excluding Mailchimp, while Online Ecosystem revenue grew 21% in Q1 or 25% excluding Mailchimp… Within Mailchimp, revenue was down slightly versus a year ago, in line with our expectations for the quarter. We continue to target double-digit growth for Mailchimp exiting fiscal 2026.

Starting the valuation analysis, traditional valuation metrics compared to the sector indicate mixed signals. PE and PEG ratios indicate an undervalued company, while EV-based ratios, like EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA, give overvalued signals.

INTU’s projected revenue remains at about 12% over the next three years, indicating a very attractive FWD P/S ratio.

Combined with its significantly higher profitability and FCF margins compared to the sector, I believe its current stock price presents an attractive opportunity.

Bottom Line

My buy ratings are always based on the risk-reward profile. INTU has an attractive profile, due to its customers-sticky business model, AI capabilities, pricing power, high switching and “trust” cost, as well as its superior financial metrics, which create a moat around the company.

Considering its EPS of approximately $1.60 in Q1’26, with a current share price of $443, compared to $0.70 EPS in Q1’25 when the price was over $680, it illustrates that the company continues to grow despite the perceived ‘AI threat’.

Note here that the company’s earnings have a seasonality effect, driven by tax-filling periods, mainly in Q2 and Q3; therefore, I anticipate further growth in the following quarters.

Overall, from my view, its risk-reward profile is considered attractive, and I recommend a BUY rating on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PIZ: Strong Returns, But High Fees And Volatility
data.description:
PIZ strategy

Invesco Dorsey Wright Developed Markets Momentum ETF (PIZ) was launched on 12/28/2007 and tracks the Dorsey Wright Developed Markets Tech Leaders™ Index. PIZ has a portfolio of 100 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.21%, and a total expense ratio of 0.80%. Dividends are paid quarterly.

As described by Invesco, the methodology starts from the largest 1,000 constituents by market capitalization in the Nasdaq Developed Markets Ex-U.S. Index. Every eligible stock is assigned a relative strength score derived from the comparison of its price return with a benchmark over a period of time. The underlying index selects approximately 100 of the best-scoring stocks, and weights them based on their scores. The index is rebalanced on a quarterly basis.

This article will use as a benchmark an ex-US developed markets index, represented by iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV).

Portfolio

PIZ has about 61% of asset value in large and mega caps, and 39% in mid-caps. The portfolio is geographically diversified; no country is weighted more than 15%. Canada comes in first position (15%), followed by Switzerland (13%), while other countries are below 9%. Compared to IDEV, PIZ massively underweights Japan.

The fund is overweight in financials (39.6%) and industrials (34.2%). Other sectors are below 12%.

The portfolio is well diversified, with moderate company-specific risk. The top 10 stock holdings, listed in the next table, represent 24.3% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 3.3%.

PIZ has the highest expense ratio, turnover, and volatility, making it the riskiest of these funds. On the upside, it is very close behind the best performer, FNDF, based on annualized return since June 2015. The other momentum fund, IMTM, lags PIZ by only 25 bps annualized, and has a better Sharpe ratio due to lower volatility.

Takeaway

Invesco Dorsey Wright Developed Markets Momentum ETF has a portfolio of 100 stocks with high price relative strength. PIZ has strong growth characteristics and is well diversified across countries and holdings, but very concentrated in financials and industrials. PIZ has performed quite well over the past years compared to a benchmark and other factor-based developed markets ETFs, but its volatility and high expense ratio warrant caution. PIZ is best-suited for tactical allocation and swing trading, where volatility can be an advantage and fees are less problematic due to limited holding periods. I think IMTM is a better alternative for investors seeking long-term exposure to the momentum factor in developed markets.

data.title: GPIX: Recent Economic Data Shows Why This Fund Should Have Another Special Year
data.description:
The effectiveness of an investing strategy usually becomes clearer over time. Financial approaches have to survive the test of time and show that these methods can work in a variety of different and more adverse market conditions. While obviously most strategies for allocating capital look good in a rising market, sell-offs can quickly show flaws in a portfolio.

Investors seeking income have far more options today than ever before. These individuals don't have to limit themselves to traditional companies known for paying consistent and substantive income; there are other, often more appealing choices, such as a covered call fund that people can invest in as well. One options-based fund that has offered both consistent income and solid total returns for over two years now is the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX).

GPIX has offered investors total returns of 61.16 percent since the fund's inception date in October of 2023. The S&P 500, which this investment closely tracks, has offered investors total returns of 69.90 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF in September of 2025, and I rated this fund a strong buy. I wanted to update my coverage of this investment for the recent economic revisions, with newer and more positive consumer spending data coming out. Today, I am reiterating my strong buy rating of GPIX. This fund is well-positioned for what likely will be a stable and slowly rising market in this kind of economic environment. The Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF should be able to offer investors both substantive income and consistent income in what should be more favorable market conditions.

The Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF has an expense ratio of .29 percent, $3.09 billion in assets under management, and a trailing yield of nearly 8.2 percent. GPIX's options strategy is to sell monthly flex options against the S&P 500 index that the fund tracks. This ETF owns actual stock. The fund uses a flexible options-based strategy that covers between 25 percent and 75 percent of the investment's portfolio, and the manager of this ETF also adjusts the strike price at which options are sold, depending on volatility levels and overall market conditions. This Goldman fund sells 1256 contracts that are taxed at 60 percent of the capital gains rate and 40 percent of the short-term income levels.

GPIX has consistently outperformed its peers and offered both solid income and total returns in line with the S&P 500 index that the investment tracks.

GPIX has offered investors total returns since the fund's inception of 61.16 percent, just 9 percent less than the S&P 500 index that this investment tracks. The ETF has also outperformed the NEOs S&P 500 Income ETF (SPYI) by nearly 10 percent, and the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) by 25 percent, during this time period. JEPI is a more defensive fund that doesn't track the S&P 500, but GPIX's ability to outperform this income-based large-cap fund is still impressive.

GPIX has also offered investors a similar risk-reward profile to other, more conservative options-based funds, such as JEPI, during market sell-offs, despite generally taking a more aggressive approach to selling options to generate income.

Even during the market sell-off in late 2024 and early 2025, GPIX outperformed the more conservatively positioned JEPI.

This Goldman fund has also continued to offer investors consistent and substantive income as well. In 2024, this ETF paid out nearly $3.69 of income and had a yield of nearly 8.15 percent. GPIX paid out $4.27 of income and had a yield of 8.14 percent during 2025.

The Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF offered investors very consistent payouts last year that ranged from $.32 to $.38 a share, showing the fund manager's ability to successfully utilize a more flexible options strategy in a variety of different market conditions to produce consistent results.

GPIX should also be well-positioned in 2026, with recent consumer spending and economic data showing the US economy remains stronger than most experts expected, and the evidence suggests Trump's tariffs are having a very minimal impact on GDP. Economic forecasts for US growth are rising, with Goldman now expecting fourth quarter GDP to rise at 2.5 percent on a year-to-year basis, compared to the consensus estimates for that time period of 2.1 percent. Analysts expect low double-digit earnings growth in the S&P 500, and earnings estimates in the large-cap tech sector are forecasted to grow by 20-25 percent, driven in significant part by the continued build-out in artificial intelligence. GPIX's overweight position in large-cap tech and more aggressive posturing in cyclical sectors should position this ETF well for the upcoming year.

All investment theses have risks, and the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF only has 2 full years of data available. GPIX has also not had to navigate an extended and more severe market sell-off. The investment is also a more aggressive fund that generally does not cover as much of the ETF's portfolio and is heavily tied to large-cap tech, so this exchange-traded fund may underperform more conservative investments if more adverse market conditions exist. GPIX also does not buy put options or protect downside risks,

No covered-call investment is likely to outperform the underlying assets these funds hold over the long term since the managers cap upside risk while leaving the downside exposed. Still, GPIX has performed well during selloffs, and the fund's operators have shown the ability to skillfully navigate through a variety of more adverse market conditions. While some investors will want more focus on distributions than the balance between payouts and total returns GPIX provides, this Goldman fund's ability to preserve the net asset value while offering consistent income should have significant appeal to most individuals who aren't simply focused on maximizing total capital gains.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: 2025 Financial Performance And Strategic Outlook
data.description:
Thesis

Royalty Pharma plc (RPRX) delivered decent financials in 2025. We saw double-digit growth in both Royalty Receipts and Portfolio Receipts. This led to very good returns on invested capital for the company. Elsewhere capital deployment remained pretty disciplined, with $4.7 billion in announced transactions across nine therapies, with $2.6 billion actually invested. We can also add $1.2 billion in share repurchases and a very impressive 7% dividend increase in early 2026. As for their portfolio, it is still expanding, which we want to see, with some upcoming clinical and regulatory developments, including Myqorzo, Tremfya, TEV-749, deucrictibant, and Trodelvy. There are also some strategic partnerships to look forward to, such as the Revolution Medicines collaboration for daraxonrasib, which I think will set the company up quite well for more continued top-line growth.

4Q25 results

I thought Royalty delivered a pretty strong set of financial results for both 4Q25 and FY25. We saw double-digit growth across some key performance metrics. Portfolio Receipts managed to reach $874 million in 4Q25 alone and $3.25 billion for the year, which represents growth of about 18% and 16%, respectively. We can mainly put this growth down to broad-based strength across a few major products such as Voranigo, Trelegy, Tremfya, and the cystic fibrosis franchise. Royalty Receipts alone grew 13% for the year, which goes to show just how durable the company's diversified royalty portfolio has become. Profitability metrics were also pretty robust, with adjusted EBITDA rising roughly 16% to $3.0 billion and Portfolio Cash Flow increasing 11% to $2.7 billion. So it would definitely seem that top-line growth is translating effectively into cash generation. Now, despite a slight decline in operating cash flow compared to 2024 from some higher development-stage funding and transaction activity, Royalty still managed to generate pretty substantial recurring cash inflows from its royalty assets.

Capital allocation also remained at the centre of value creation in 2025. Royalty deployed about $2.6 billion into new royalty transactions, which expanded the portfolio with both late-stage and development-stage assets. With the most notable asset being the partnership with Revolution Medicines on daraxonrasib. Elsewhere, the company returned a decent chunk of capital to shareholders via $1.2 billion in share repurchases and $512 million in dividends. They also raised their quarterly dividend by roughly 7% entering 2026.

As for guidance, management expects 2026 Portfolio Receipts to fall somewhere in the range of $3.28 to $3.43 billion, which would imply continued growth of about 3% to 8% in Royalty Receipts even before we add contributions from new deals. So, if we take this outlook, combined with multiple expected clinical and regulatory catalysts upcoming, as I'll explain, I think Royalty is pretty well positioned for another year of steady cash flow expansion.

Portfolio

I think it's worth taking a look at how Royalty operates its diversified portfolio, since it wouldn't be your simple pharma company. You see, they operate a portfolio of royalty interests tied to approved and late-stage pharmaceutical products, which means they don't actually manufacture or market drugs themselves but instead earn revenue from sales generated by their partners' medicines. So the hard part for them is picking the right products rather than developing them. The company makes money mainly via Royalty Receipts, which are contractual percentages of net sales from certain blockbuster therapies where they see success. These could be for cystic fibrosis, cancer, immunology, or even rare diseases. Now, Royalty Receipts would be linked to milestones and other contractual payments from regulatory approvals or commercial performance.

As you would expect, Royalty Pharma generally acquires these revenue streams by providing upfront capital to biotech/pharmaceutical companies, either to fund R&D or support general commercialisation. Sometimes they purchase existing royalty rights altogether. Either way, the outcome is long-term exposure to product sales without bearing the operational risks of drug development or commercialisation. I put together a quick table below to summarise the biggest contributors in their portfolio.

Synthetic Royalties

A big talking point on the call was that synthetic royalties are starting to emerge as a very reliable growth engine for Royalty. For those that don't know, synthetic royalty is a financial arrangement where a company receives upfront or staged funding in exchange for a future percentage of sales from a drug or therapy, but without actually owning the product, so it essentially creates a royalty-like cash flow through a structured deal rather than a traditional licensing or acquisition.

For Royalty, the advantages have mainly come from accelerating market adoption and the company's increasing deal leadership, according to management. Last year was Royalty's strongest year ever for synthetic royalty transactions, in which they managed to execute four synthetic deals totalling over $2 billion. For context, this was five times the level seen in 2020, and it's also worth noting that for the first time, synthetic royalties actually exceeded traditional royalty investments in announced transaction value. Management also told us that the overall synthetic royalty market reached a record hefty $4.7 billion in 2025, growing about 50% year over year; however, it still represents only about 5% of total biopharma funding, which would imply a substantial runway for expansion in the near term.

I think we can put this appeal down to the structural advantages. You see, synthetic royalties provide nondilutive capital and avoid a lot of operational restrictions typical of debt. On one side, they give biopharma companies upfront capital without actually issuing equity, so there's no shareholder dilution. They also cut down on the covenants, repayment schedules, or operational constraints that usually come with debt, so it allows management to run the business and develop drugs with far greater flexibility.

They can also be customised to the Royalty's needs, which makes them increasingly attractive to biotech boards and executive teams as a very good financing tool rather than a niche option. For Royalty's pipeline, the data clearly shows a shift. They have growing volumes of high-quality, in-depth reviews and what would seem to be repeat engagement from biopharma companies that are considering synthetic royalties as part of their capital structure. Management also explicitly framed synthetics as a long-term paradigm shift in biotech funding. They also seemed to have confidence that synthetics will be a pretty important and durable driver of capital deployment and earnings growth in the coming few years.

Valuation

When it comes to a valuation, Royalty stock would appear quite attractively priced relative to both the sector and its own history. The stock trades at about 9x forward non-GAAP earnings, which is about a 50% discount to the Health Care sector median. I think we can put this down to a rather conservative view from the market due to the dependency of their revenue, despite the fact that we're seeing a pretty stable, cash-generating royalty model. On a cash flow basis, shares also look quite compelling, with a trailing price-to-cash-flow multiple of roughly 8x versus more than 15x for the sector, which goes to show the strength of its recurring royalty income. Elsewhere, headline revenue multiples remain rather elevated, with around 9x forward EV to sales; however, this is pretty typical for high-margin royalty businesses with minimal operating costs. Overall, I think we have a nice combination of a low forward earnings multiple, solid cash flow valuation, and a near-2% dividend yield, which puts the stock priced more like a slow-growth pharma company rather than a capital-light royalty platform with steady mid-single-digit growth.

One thing that does come to mind with their valuation is that we usually see royalty companies command decent sector premiums. So, Royalty here is currently trading at a discount for the most part due to market scepticism rather than weak fundamentals, for me. I think investors are staying cautious about patent expirations and product concentration risk, particularly with respect to some of their more mature blockbuster drugs that face eventual generic or biosimilar competition in the near term.

Looking ahead

As for key milestones to look out for, Royalty has a pretty robust pipeline that could drive future growth. The key clinical readouts would include the Phase 3 results for Myqorzo, daraxonrasib, pelacarsen, Trodelvy, litifilimab, and deucrictibants. On the regulatory front, investors should also keep an eye on potential FDA approvals for TEV-749, tividenofusp alfa, zidesamtinib, and Trodelvy, as well as an upcoming FDA filing for neladalkib and obexelimab.

In terms of stock price, Royalty currently trades in the $45 range, a figure approaching the 52-week high. The stock has seen very steady, slow growth over the past year, and I do feel there is more upside ahead after what we saw on this earnings, which is why I'm buy-rated.

data.title: First Eagle High Yield Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

During the fourth quarter, the municipal bond market continued to rebound from the challenges of the first half of 2025.

Record-setting bond issuance continued to be met by strong demand, enabling muni indexes to post positive returns for the quarter and the year. The S&P Municipal Bond High Yield Index gained 1.5% during the fourth quarter, while the S&P Municipal Yield Index, which includes bonds across the quality spectrum, rose 1.7% and the S&P Short Duration Municipal Yield Index advanced 0.6%. Those indexes were up a respective 3.3%, 3.5% and 4.3% in full-year 2025. For context, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index gained 1.1% during the quarter and 7.3% in the year. 1

Market Able to Absorb Record Levels of Muni Issuance

Though the pace of muni bond issuance eased somewhat in the fourth quarter, the $100 billion-plus of new issuance during the period was enough to drive the market to a new annual high of $580 billion, about 1.3% above the previous record set in 2024. It's worth noting that the vast majority of issuance during the year was new capital, with refunding activity accounting for only about 12%. 2 Lower levels of refunding transactions suggest fewer bonds are being called, which can help support valuations for the market as a whole.

We were not surprised to see the municipal bond curve steepen considerably during 2025. The flood of issuance—high yield muni issuance in particular, given its long-dated nature, generally provides a steepening impulse to the yield curve—pushed long yields higher, although Federal Reserve policy rate cuts weighed on the short end of the muni curve. 3 The US Treasury curve also steepened over the year as term premia returned to the Treasury market after being mostly negative for nearly a decade. 4

There are a few factors we believe have contributed to the ongoing surge in issuance. After sitting on the sidelines during the 2022–23 rate-hike period, municipalities have a pent-up need to issue paper as the benefits of Covid-era federal funding and post-pandemic tax receipts wane. Meanwhile, pretty much every capital project costs more today from the impact of inflation across inputs like steel, concrete and lumber, and increased costs for civil engineering, skilled and unskilled labor, etc. Further, potential legislative threats to muni bond tax treatment in the first half of the year—since alleviated—likely pulled forward some issuance.

Fortunately, the US government shutdown that began on October 1 did not have the same chilling effect on demand as the Liberation Day tariff announcements in April, and massive issuance was met by robust demand throughout the fourth quarter. Flows into municipal bond mutual funds and exchange traded funds ended the year strong, amounting to about $23 billion in the fourth quarter, which represented more than one-third of full-year 2025 flows. 5

The Fed's dovish tilt in the second half of the year has also supported demand for muni bonds. Though it was cut off from official federal agency data during the government shutdown, the Fed followed up its September rate cut with another in October. The catch-up in economic data flow, after federal employees returned to work in mid-November, did little to change the central bank's viewpoint, which seemed to center more on a weakening labor market than inflation concerns, and an additional December cut brought the federal funds rate down to 3.50–3.75% to end the year. 6

The Fed appears well-positioned to wait and see how the economy evolves, and the dot plot of rate projections published in December points to only one additional 25 basis point cut in 2026. 7 That said, the Trump administration's very public desire for lower rates and conflict with Fed Chair Powell—highlighted by early-January reports that the Department of Justice had opened a criminal investigation into Powell related to the renovation of the Fed's headquarters—suggest that whoever replaces Powell when his term as Fed chair ends in May 2026 is likely to be more dovish than the average governor. In a world of increasingly lower floating-rate yields, investors may increasingly view fixed-rate municipal bonds as a more appealing cash equivalent.

Fundamentals Remain Steady

The initial estimate of third quarter GDP for the US came in at 4.3%, up from 3.8% in the second quarter and well above the consensus estimate of 3.3%. While growth in the fourth quarter is likely to be negatively impacted by the government shutdown, fiscal stimulus from the 2025 tax bill and potential monetary stimulus should be supportive of economic activity in 2026, to the ongoing benefit of municipal issuer fundamentals. 8

Municipalities entered 2025 in strong fiscal condition, and issuer fundamentals continue to be supportive. State budgets for fiscal 2026 overall reflect a healthy environment, and general fund balances remain well above the historical average even as they continue to ease from 2023's peak. Though state general fund revenue has fallen off the record pace of fiscal 2021 and 2022 as the impact of Covid-era relief waned, it has continued to grow, and modest revenue gains are expected in fiscal 2026. Budgets enacted to date suggest flat general fund spending in 2026, and most states plan to maintain or increase the size of their rainy-day fund—many of which are already at nominal highs—in anticipation of future needs. 9

Another sign of fiscal strength can be found in improved pension funding, as the aggregate median ratio for local-government pensions climbed to 80% in fiscal 2024 from 78% in fiscal 2022. While this can be attributed in part to market performance, local governments have increased contributions and tweaked their benefit structures, demonstrating improved funding discipline and better long-term sustainability. 10

Overall, muni bond ratings activity has been positive in 2025, but not by much: Positive activity (including both upgrades and favorable outlook revisions) outpaced negative activity at a rate of 1.4x year to date through November. 11 Both defaults and first-time distressed debt remained very low in 2025. 12

Start of a Turnaround?

Muni bond performance in 2025 was a tale of two halves, and we're encouraged by the asset class's momentum in the second half. It seems likely to us that the factors driving recent performance and fund flows—credit stability, certainty around tax treatment, an accommodative Fed and relatively benign tariff impacts—will continue to support the asset class, even in what is expected to be another year of robust issuance.

As a team, we remain focused on seeking to provide a high level of tax-free current income through bottom-up security selection complemented by an active trading strategy and the prudent application of leverage. This approach, combined with consistent fund inflows, has enabled us to increase the interest income generated by the portfolio in 2025, and we have passed this along to shareholders in the form of a higher monthly distribution rate. 13

Portfolio Review

High Yield Municipal Fund A Shares (FEHAX) (without sales charge*) posted a return of -0.25% in fourth quarter 2025. The Fund underperformed the S&P Municipal Yield Index in the period.

While municipal bonds issued by Puerto Rico account for more than 8% of the benchmark index, we take a highly selective approach to buying paper issued by the territory. Investing in US territories entails unique legal and political risks not typical of the broader municipal bond market and must be approached with additional diligence.

Revenue bonds backed by toll roads were among the few Puerto Rico issues that have met our investment criteria, and they were a strong contributor to fourth-quarter performance. The roads in question connect several of the island's key economic hubs. Issued with a BBB rating in 2024, these bonds were upgraded to BBB+ in December by Fitch, citing positive traffic and revenue trends supportive of stronger financial metrics, and price appreciation resulted.

In contrast with our holdings for Puerto Rico, healthcare represents a significant exposure for the Fund. Senior care and senior living are areas within healthcare where we think fundamental analysis may help identify attractive bonds that stand to benefit from the "silver tsunami" of Americans who will retire by 2030. While this sector has recovered from Covid-19 disruptions, it still accounted for a meaningful portion of the first-time muni defaults in 2025, which is why we prioritize the bonds of well-managed senior living facilities that are located in growing states and are less dependent on government-subsidized residents. Among the largest contributors to fourth-quarter performance were bonds issued by Sanctuary LTC, which owns a portfolio of more than 20 nonprofit lifecare facilities in attractive markets in Texas, Oklahoma and Colorado.

Bonds related to the Brightline passenger rail project in Florida comprised the biggest detractors to performance in the fourth quarter. Brightline, which is backed by private equity firm Fortress Investment Group (FIG), is the only privately owned and operated intercity railroad in the US. It began service in Florida in 2018 and has steadily increased its footprint along the east coast of the state from Miami to Orlando and has plans to expand its network from Orlando to Tampa.

These bonds came under fire in 2025 and were subject to downgrades from rating agencies citing lower-than-expected ridership and higher costs as well as dwindling cash reserves and other liquidity concerns. In July, Brightline announced that it would defer its mid-month interest payment on certain tranches of debt, news to which markets reacted very negatively, which we believe was an overreaction. Outstanding Brightline bonds continue to trade at deep discounts.

Despite Brightline's recent difficulties, we remain constructive on both the project and the bonds. The negative market sentiment largely has been priced into the bonds, in our view, substantially reducing our downside risk in 2026 while enhancing our upside potential. Meanwhile, recent trends at the company have been bullish.

Operational improvements in 2025—including the addition of 30 new train cars and a complete schedule revamp to improve network optimization—have put Brightline on the verge of finally generating positive monthly cash flow. This operational momentum will have a direct impact on the ability of Fortress Investments (Brightline's owner) to attract equity investors and raise substantial additional funds to improve Brightline's capital structure and pay down higher coupon debt as well as deferred interest. We have been told that potential equity investors are spending significant time and money on due diligence efforts.

In January 2026, Brightline announced the appointment of Nicolas Petrovic as CEO of Brightline Holdings LLC. Petrovic has more than 25 years of rail experience, with leadership positions across Europe and the Middle East, including with Eurostar, Siemens (SIEGY) France and, most recently, Etihad Rail Mobility. In our view, Brightline's ability to lure such a tenured and respected CEO to Florida at this stage of his career speaks volumes about the company's prospects going forward.

In short, we believe outstanding Brightline bonds have been oversold and that the combination of operational momentum, new leadership and a likely equity infusion suggests substantial recovery potential.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

During the transition period, it is expected that the Fund will not be as invested in income-producing securities that are exempt from regular federal income taxes as will be the case once the transition is complete. Funds whose investments are concentrated in a specific industry or sector may be subject to a higher degree of risk than funds whose investments are diversified and may not be suitable for all investors. Investments in bonds are subject to interest-rate risk and can lose principal value when interest rates rise, while they typically increase their principal values when interest rates decline. Bonds are also subject to credit risk, in which the bond issuer may fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner, or that negative perception of the issuer's ability to make such payments may cause the price of that bond to decline. The Fund may invest in high yield, fixed income securities that, at the time of purchase, are non-investment grade. High yield, lower rated securities involve greater price volatility and present greater risks than high rated fixed income securities. High yield securities are rated lower than investment-grade securities because there is a greater possibility that the issuer may be unable to make interest and principal payments on those securities. High yield securities involve greater risk than higher rated securities and portfolios that invest in them may be subject to greater levels of credit and liquidity risk than portfolios that do not. Municipal bonds are subject to credit risk, interest rate risk, liquidity risk, and call risk . However, the obligations of some municipal issuers may not be enforceable through the exercise of traditional creditors' rights. The reorganization under federal bankruptcy laws of a municipal bond issuer may result in the bonds being cancelled without payment or repaid only in part, or in delays in collecting principal and interest.

Definitions

Federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight on an uncollateralized basis. A yield curve is a graphical representation of interest rates on debt of equal credit quality across a range of maturities. Gross domestic product ('GDP') measures the total value of all economic output in goods and services for an economy. Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are listed investment vehicles that seek to provide exposure to a benchmark, index or actively managed strategy.

S&P Municipal Yield Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of high yield and investment grade municipal bonds. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. S&P Short Duration Municipal Yield Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of high yield and investment grade municipal bonds with maturities of one to 12 years. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. S&P Municipal Bond High Yield Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of bonds in the S&P Municipal Bond Index that are not rated or whose ratings are below investment grade. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market in the US, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, fixed-rate agency MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM passthroughs), ABS, and CMBS. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income.

Indexes are unmanaged and do not incur management fees or other operating expenses. One cannot invest directly in an index.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: What I Learned From Warren Buffett's Japan Investment
data.description:
BRK’s Japan Investment: A Masterclass

I last analyzed Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) on Jan 6, 2026. That article was titled "Berkshire Hathaway: Buffett Retires, But Triple Compounding Continues." It was triggered by the leadership transition at the company and rated the stock as a hold. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around the stock, both in terms of macroeconomics and also company specifics. Here, I will draw your attention to two, one in each category, as I see it. For the former category, I want to focus on the change in Japan’s Nikkei 225 index and also BRK’s Japan positions. For the latter category, I want to detail my thoughts on the potential impacts of these changes on BRK’s next earnings report scheduled on Feb 28, 2026.

Let me start with the recent political and economic changes in Japan and their impacts on BRK’s Japan investments. The country’s Nikkei index has spent years in a consolidation pattern around the 40,000 level. With Sanae Takaichi’s victory in the country’s snap election earlier this month, the index has decidedly moved above the 50,000 psychological level and touched on a record of over 58,000. As many of you may know, Buffett has been accumulating shares of several Japanese trading houses (or sogo shosha)—and also praising the attractiveness of their valuation and profitability metrics—for years. As an example, his comments back in 2023 are quoted below.

Yahoo! News: On his 90th birthday in August 2020, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) announced initial stakes of just over 5% in each of Japan’s five largest trading houses — Mitsubishi (MUFG), Itochu (ITOCF), Mitsui (MITSF), Marubeni (MARUF), and Sumitomo (SSUMF). During a 2023 trip to Japan, Buffett explained his rationale in a CNBC interview: “I was confounded we could buy into these companies and have an earnings yield of 14%. The dividends actually grew 70% during that time, and the people were investing their money at a quarter of a percent or nothing.”

Over the years, Buffett has continued increasing BRK’s position in these stocks. By the end of 2024, these holdings were valued at approximately $23.5 billion with a cost basis of less than $14 billion, per my estimate. With the massive rally in the Nikkei index and these companies share prices since my last writing, my estimate of the current market value for BRK’s Japan positions is around $40 billion now.

Next, I will explain why A) these investments gave me a masterclass in global capital allocation and currency-hedged value investing and B) how these investments showcase the structural advantage in BRK’s capital allocation.

BRK Stock: My Lesson About Value Investing

Looking forward, I see these investments offer excellent prospects for BRK. In the immediate term, I expect them to further increase BRK’s shareholder equity by a noticeable amount in the upcoming FQ4 earnings report (ER). To wit, the chart below shows the consolidated balance sheet for Berkshire as of the end of its FQ3 (September 30, 2025). As seen, the company reported significant growth in all its liquid assets. Cash and cash equivalents rose from $44,333 million to $72,156 million, while short-term investments in U.S. Treasury Bills increased from $286,472 million to $305,367 million. Finally, equity securities (where the above Japanese positions are reflected) increased to $283,241 despite the sizable trimming of several of its US equity holdings in FQ3.

Looking further ahead, I believe Buffett’s investments in Japan will continue providing excellent prospects for strong cash flow and also asset appreciation while he enjoys his retirement. These positions offer a combination of healthy growth, robust profitability, and generous dividend yields. As an example, the table below provides a summary of their dividend metrics. As a group, these companies provide an average yield of around 2.1%. Thus, the dividend income alone would be more than $800 million for BRK in 2026. Also note that these companies have been growing their dividends at double digits on average for the past 3-year and 5-year periods. For the past 3 years, the median growth rate has been about 11.5%. At this growth rate, the dividend income from this group would exceed $1 billion in about 2 years. This is not shabby for an investment with a cost basis of about $14 billion only, per my estimate above, a living example of value and compounding.

But the beauty of this masterclass does not end here.

BRK Stock: Lesson About Capital Allocation

To me, these investments are virtually an arbitrage masterfully executed by Buffett: the arbitrage between borrowing at near-zero interest rates in yen to purchase high-yielding and high-growth assets. As individual investors, you and I could also buy these Japanese stocks in the open market. But you and I won’t be able to enjoy the structural advantage in BRK’s capital allocation strategies. In his 2023 interview mentioned earlier, Buffett also revealed he’s been financing the bet with low-interest yen bonds, borrowing at about 0.5% while collecting much higher yields from dividends and buybacks. “We’ll be in these stocks 10, 20 years,” he said. I will be in these stocks for 20 years too if I have access to these borrowing rates. A lesson I learned about family finances is that when borrowing rates are lower than risk-free rates, I should borrow as much as I can for as long as I can.

Japan’s bond yields have increased substantially since then, and BRK’s overall borrowing rates are not as ridiculously low as 0.5%. As an example, the chart below (taken from GuruFocus) shows a detailed calculation of the annualized effective interest rate on debt for Berkshire Hathaway for the quarter ending in September 2025. This calculation was performed by dividing the annualized interest expense of $5,044 million by the average total debt across the two most recent quarters. Specifically, the total debt from June 2025, which was $127,020 million, is averaged with the September 2025 total debt of $127,243 million, resulting in a mean value of $127,131 million. The annualized interest expense was then divided by this average debt to arrive at the final effective interest rate of 3.97%. It is a lot higher than 0.5% but still lower than the 10-year US treasury bond rates (which hover around 4.1% as of this writing).

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Before closing, there are a couple of downside risks worth noting. BRK’s valuation is certainly not cheap. As an example, the chart below shows its price-to-book (P/B) ratio in the past 10 years. The ratio now stands at 1.53x, noticeably above the average ratio of 1.41x. Even after factoring in the impact of the above Japanese investments, my estimate of its P/B ratio is still above the historical average.

As another risk that is specific to my thesis here, these sogo shosha can be sensitive to commodity cycles. These sogo shosha are categorized as diversified conglomerates and are often described as Japan’s BRK. However, just like BRK itself, these companies rely on the commodity cycles to a good degree via their energy exposure (just like BRK). The cyclical shift in the energy and raw material prices could impact their profitability quite sensitively in my model. With the new Takaichi administration, investors also need to consider the China factor: China is Japan’s top trading partner. The new administration’s diplomatic and trading agenda with China could impact the supply-demand dynamics for the key energy and raw material commodities such as iron ore, coal, and LNG.

All told, this revisit to BRK is triggered by two recent catalysts: the geopolitical change in Japan and BRK’s upcoming earnings report for FQ4. These changes have led me to see increased equity valuation for BRK’s investments in several Japanese trading houses. I expect to see a noticeable impact in the FQ4 ER. Looking past FQ4, I see good odds for these investments to keep providing excellent return prospects for BRK investors thanks to the combination of BRK’s structural advantage in capital allocation and the healthy economics of these holdings.

Patrick Kelly, CFA

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross- currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure. Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

The Alger AI Enablers & Adopters ETF (ALAI) underperformed the S&P 500 Index during the third quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Western Digital Corporation (WDC), and Natera, Inc. (NTRA) were among the top contributors to performance.

Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.

Western Digital is a hard disk drive (HDD) storage company benefiting from rising hyperscaler data-center investment and the continued proliferation of data. The HDD industry is highly consolidated, with only two scaled manufacturers, and Western Digital holds a leading market position. The business has structurally shifted toward cloud customers as consumer exposure has declined, with cloud representing the majority of Western Digital’s revenue. Importantly, industry participants have emphasized capital discipline—prioritizing higher areal density (i.e., more terabytes per drive) rather than adding significant unit capacity—which supports a healthier supply/demand balance and improved profitability. During the quarter, shares contributed positively as the company reported operating results that validated improving fundamentals, including strong revenue and earnings growth driven by higher unit volumes, better-than-guided drive density, favorable pricing, and lower cost per terabyte that helped expand margins.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Nebius Group (NBIS), Sea Limited (SE), and Meta Platforms Inc. (META) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Sea Limited is a global consumer internet company operating three core businesses: Garena, a digital entertainment platform known for developing and publishing online games; Shopee, a leading e-commerce platform in Southeast Asia and Taiwan; and SeaMoney, which offers digital financial services including mobile wallets and payment processing. The company has benefited from strong structural growth in its e-commerce and payments business, along with its gaming business returning to stabilization after years of decline. Shares detracted from performance as investors questioned the pace and durability of Sea’s profitability improvement. Specifically, its largest e-commerce competitor in Latin America lowered free-shipping thresholds, which may increase competitive intensity and pressure margins in the near- term.

Meta is the world’s largest social media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (CapEx) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

data.title: Li Auto Relegated To Playing Catch-Up
data.description:
For most Chinese NEV makers, 2026 began with a stock rally on potential and actual easing of trade restrictions abroad (plus some policy support). Li Auto (LI)(LAAOF), however, underwhelmed and stood out for the wrong reason, claiming the dubious title of last year's second-most profitable short in Asia Pacific.

With deliveries down year-on-year for the eighth straight month, Wall Street sentiment is cooling. And I can't disagree; Li Auto is looking increasingly unappealing.

Li Auto's dethroning

Li Auto had been the model startup - carving out its own lane in a crowded market by betting on extended range EVs (EREVs) and positioning itself as a premium family brand. It worked like a charm: revenue climbed steadily, and high selling prices supported margins. Its vehicle margin, over 20% in Q3 2024, was among the best in the industry.

In 2024 growth slowed, then came 2025. For the full year, Li Auto delivered 406,343 vehicles, down 19%, which was just about half of the original target. Falling ASP and rising costs have squeezed margins further. Vehicle margin dropped to 15% in Q3 2025.

Share price is down more than 20% over the past year.

I see few reasons for what went wrong.

Strategy aspect

Strategy is a big one. Even as demand kept moving toward pure BEVs and advanced PHEVs, Li Auto stuck to EREVs. Warning signs of early 2024 the L-series began losing steam and delivery guidance was cut - were either missed or ignored. Instead of pivoting then, the company stayed the course: EREVs still accounted for 97.8% of total deliveries in 2025.

Its first BEVs arrived only in September 2025 - almost 18 months after those first cracks. By then, the pivot was already behind schedule, plus production capacity for the new i-series lagged at the very time it was needed most. To make things worse, those BEV models underwhelmed, and Li Mega even had to be recalled, which cut 4.3 percentage points off gross margin in Q3 2025.

Li Auto's interim solution for demand issues has been price cutting and discounting. A more structural fix, 'product realignment,' that is aimed at clearer product identity, faster overseas expansion, and shorter platform iteration cycles, is in the works. But whether that's going to be enough to reignite growth will depend on management - whose credibility has already taken a hit - as well as competition that remains as brutal as ever.

Market factor

To be sure, China's NEV market - which accounts for more than 50% of new car sales - is still vibrant compared to the rest of the world, but the days of hypergrowth seem over. Growth is expected to slow sharply: NEV sales may increase by about 15% in 2026 (down from 28% last year) and vehicle exports by just 4% (versus 21%).

The main reason is changing consumer spending patterns. Chinese buyers have been gravitating toward budget NEV options for some time. Affordability is now under more pressure. Although the trade-in subsidy has been extended, the tax exemption on NEVs has been halved since the beginning of the year, and local incentives are being scaled back due to depleted budgets.

Competition factor

Industry growth may be slowing, but competition certainly isn't. Persistent price wars are destroying profitability - average gross margin sank to 1.8% in December 2025. Then there is consolidation: the top 10 NEV makers now control 95% of the market, up from 60-70% a couple of years ago.

Even in this environment, though, startups like NIO and XPeng, along with newer entrants like Xiaomi and Huawei's HIMA, have delivered strong 2025 performances. That alone must have jolted Li Auto to action since they all target the same higher-end segment.

Its legacy EREVs have been losing ground to PHEVs and BEVs from mature competitors like BYD and Geely, while its own BEVs have struggled to gain traction. The natural result is a loss of market share: Li Auto's share of the NEV market slipped from 4.7% in 2024 to 3.2% in 2025 - to be level with Xiaomi, a company that began deliveries a full five years behind.

Valuation

On sales multiples, especially the EV/sales basis (accounting for Li Auto's cash pile), the stock looks cheap - historically and more affordable than closest peers NIO and XPeng and even heavyweight BYD.

Comparing based on trailing earnings is less useful at the moment given Li Auto's net loss in Q3 2025. A forward P/E of 109.51x reflects analyst expectations for full 2025 earnings to be only slightly positive.
